title: Howe Duality Over Finite Fields
speaker: Sophie Kriz
abstract: In this talk, I will completely describe the decomposition of an oscillator representation under the tensor embedding of a product of a symplectic and orthogonal group in the case of finite fields via a correspondence proposed by Roger Howe. Applications include a proof of the rank conjecture and a very stable case of the character sum conjecture of Shamgar Gurevich and Roger Howe. My approach is based on studying the endomorphism algebras from the point of view of interpolation of tensor categories.
tags: Special Joint IAS/PU Group Theory Afternoon

proof_stages.label: Finite-field oscillator and type-I dual-pair setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls oscillator representations from the Heisenberg group, the usual theta-correspondence framework, and then specializes over odd finite fields to type-I pairs (Sp(V),O(W,B)) with W decomposed into hyperbolic and anisotropic parts.

proof_stages.label: Stable ranges and top eta/zeta correspondences
proof_stages.summary: After defining the symplectic and orthogonal stable ranges, the speaker states the Gurevich-Howe eta injections from orthogonal to symplectic representations and the symmetric zeta injections in the opposite stable range, each describing the top part of the restricted oscillator representation.

proof_stages.label: Stable-layer formula from invariant endomorphism algebras
proof_stages.summary: The full restricted oscillator representation in stable range is decomposed into layers indexed by isotropic dimensions, and the proof is organized via the star-product model of End(omega), invariant endomorphism algebras, reflection/idempotent operators, matrix-algebra substructures, and a dimension count.

proof_stages.label: Lusztig-symbol description of the correspondences
proof_stages.summary: The eta and zeta maps are made explicit in Lusztig’s parametrization by semisimple dual-group data and unipotent symbols, with the lift altering only the symbol attached to the relevant plus- or minus-one eigenspace.

proof_stages.label: Metastable interpolation and virtual cancellation
proof_stages.summary: The gap between stable ranges is handled by interpolating the stable algebraic formulas in rank, interpreting zero or negative specializations as vanishing or virtual representations, and cancelling them to replace signed parabolic inductions by alternating sums that give genuine all-range decompositions.

proof_stages.label: Consequences for rank and commutator character sums
proof_stages.summary: The explicit correspondences are applied to the rank conjecture for symplectic groups and to weak Shale/Ore-type character-ratio cancellation statements, where small representations coming from eta images control the main contributions.

examples.name: Classical local-field theta correspondence
examples.note: The comparison with the cases over C, R, and non-Archimedean local fields illustrates the familiar bijective theta-correspondence picture against which the finite-field behavior is contrasted.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker recalls the classical local-field theta correspondence as background, stating its oscillator-representation setup and bijective decomposition without carrying out a concrete computation or construction in a particular local-field case

examples.name: Transvection-type worst case for character-ratio cancellation
examples.note: A particular nonidentity element is used to show that a single even-orthogonal eta image gives nonzero limits, so the split and nonsplit contributions must both be included to cancel.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the transvection-type worst case by computing the partial character-ratio sums for split and nonsplit even orthogonal sources as (-1)^m and (-1)^{m+1}, showing their cancellation

passes.reason: a transvection is a minimal nontrivial unipotent worst case used to show one even eta image need not cancel

examples.name: Odd zeta recipe by adding minus-one eigenvalues
examples.note: For a stable zeta lift from Sp_{2n} to O_{2m+1}, the speaker illustrates the Lusztig-parameter rule by adding minus-one eigenvalues and appending one entry to the relevant symbol.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly runs the odd zeta recipe by adding minus-one eigenvalues to the semisimple parameter, tracking the changed centralizer and the corresponding modification of the Lusztig symbol
